cveMetadata.datePublished: EMBARGOED — publish only after fix confirmed deployed

cna.title: Hearth: Redirect URI registration accepts insecure URIs — open redirect and code theft via fragment injection

descriptions.value: Hearth's OAuth 2.0 client registration endpoint performs only length and emptiness checks on redirect URIs (`src/identity/validation.rs:validate_redirect_uri`). It does not reject `http://` scheme URIs, URIs containing fragment components (`#`), wildcard patterns, or non-https schemes such as `javascript:` or `data:`. As a result: (1) An attacker who can register or modify a client can supply an insecure `http://` redirect URI, causing authorization codes to be delivered over unencrypted transport where they are susceptible to interception. (2) If a redirect URI containing a fragment component is registered and used, the authorization server appends query parameters after the fragment anchor, creating a fragment injection condition in which the authorization code may be exposed in the browser's location bar, history, or via Referer header leakage to embedded resources. This is consistent with the attack classes described in RFC 9700 §4.1 and §4.2, and violates RFC 6749 §3.1.2 which states redirect URIs MUST NOT contain a fragment component.

supportingMedia.value: Hearth's redirect URI validation (validate_redirect_uri) checks only length and non-empty constraints. It does not enforce https://, reject fragment components, or reject wildcard patterns. This allows registration of insecure URIs that enable open redirect and code theft via Referer or fragment injection.

descriptions.description: CWE-601: URL Redirection to Untrusted Site ('Open Redirect') — weak redirect URI validation allows registering http:// URIs that direct authorization codes to unencrypted or attacker-controlled endpoints.
CWE-184: Incomplete List of Disallowed Inputs — the redirect URI allowlist validation omits security-critical constraints (scheme enforcement, fragment rejection, wildcard rejection).

scenarios.value: Attacker registers a client (or modifies an existing client) with an http:// or fragment-bearing redirect URI, then tricks a victim user into initiating an OAuth flow, causing the authorization code to be delivered to an attacker-controlled or unencrypted endpoint.

references.name: RFC 9700 §4.1 — Redirect URI validation; §4.2 — Credential Leakage via Referrer Headers
RFC 6749 §3.1.2 — Redirect URI: MUST NOT contain a fragment component
RFC 6819 — OAuth 2.0 Threat Model and Security Considerations §4.1.5

timeline.value: Finding identified via internal security audit (SecurityResearcher agent, HEA-481).
Embargo lifted and advisory published after fix confirmed deployed.

credits.value: Hearth internal security audit — SecurityResearcher agent

workarounds.value: As an interim measure: (1) Only register `https://` redirect URIs for all OAuth clients — do not use `http://` except for loopback addresses (`http://127.0.0.1` or `http://[::1]`) as permitted by RFC 8252 for native clients. (2) Do not register URIs with fragment components. (3) Audit existing client registrations and remove any non-https or fragment-bearing URIs. (4) Restrict who can register or modify OAuth clients to trusted administrators only.

supportingMedia.value: Only register https:// redirect URIs. Audit and remove existing insecure registrations. Restrict client registration to trusted administrators pending server-side enforcement.

x_internalNotes.embargoLifts: After fix confirmed deployed — NOT solely on merge of HEA-501.
x_internalNotes.ctoApprovalStatus: APPROVED — 2026-05-15 (CTO: brad@inteletech.io). CVSS 7.5 confirmed (AC:L because attacker just needs a registered client; no score changes required). Code evidence line numbers reflect pre-fix snapshot; validate_redirect_uri is now fully hardened in feature/more-gap-fixes (fragment, wildcard, scheme enforcement added).

codeEvidence.description: Pre-fix state: validate_redirect_uri checked only non-empty and MAX_REDIRECT_URI_LENGTH. NOTE: Fix is now in feature/more-gap-fixes — the function now enforces: no fragment (#), no wildcards (*), https:// required (http:// only for localhost per RFC 8252), dangerous schemes rejected. Line numbers reflect pre-merge snapshot.
files: src/identity/validation.rs (pre-fix) — validate_redirect_uri: only empty/length checks, no scheme or fragment enforcement
src/identity/engine.rs (pre-fix) — validation::validate_redirect_uri(uri)? at client registration; no scheme check
src/identity/engine.rs (pre-fix) — same weak validation at update_client
src/identity/validation.rs (post-fix ~line 279) — now enforces fragment, wildcard, scheme rules (https/localhost/custom-scheme)

x_internalNotes.specReference: RFC 6749 §3.1.2 — 'The redirection endpoint URI MUST NOT include a fragment component.' RFC 9700 §4.1 — 'Servers MUST compare redirect URIs using simple string comparison as defined in [RFC3986] Section 6.2.1. ... Authorization servers MUST require clients to register redirect URIs.' RFC 9700 §4.1.3 — recommendation to reject http:// URIs except for loopback.
attackScenarios: Scenario A (insecure scheme): Attacker with client registration access registers redirect_uri=http://attacker.example.com/cb. Victim initiates OAuth flow; authorization code delivered in cleartext over HTTP, interceptable by network observers.

Scenario B (fragment injection): Registered URI contains a '#' character. During redirect, browser re-parses the URI causing the code parameter to be treated as or appear after a fragment anchor, leaking it to Referer headers sent to resources embedded in the callback page.

Scenario C (wildcard/prefix): If future code changes weaken the exact-match check, a URI like https://app.example.com could match https://app.example.com.evil.com due to prefix confusion — the current exact-match is correct but the registration check should prevent dangerous URIs from being stored at all.
